title: RAKF Security: Users, Profiles & Privilege Analysis
description: Explore the RAKF security system on TK5. View users and profiles tables, understand access control, identify misconfigurations, and trace authorization decisions.

steps.title: RAKF Architecture Overview
steps.screen:
RAKF — Resource Access Control Facility (v1.2.6)

RAKF is a RACF-like SAF for MVS 3.8j.
All security calls (RACDEF, RACINIT, RACHECK)
route through ICHSFR00.

Two in-memory tables control everything:
  SYS1.SECURE.CNTL(USERS)    — who can log on
  SYS1.SECURE.CNTL(PROFILES) — what they can access

Tables are reloadable:
  S RAKFUSER   — reload users
  S RAKFPROF   — reload profiles
steps.expected: Understand that RAKF uses text-based tables, not a database like real RACF.
steps.hint: Unlike z/OS RACF, there are no ADDUSER/PERMIT commands. You edit text files directly.

steps.title: Browse RAKF Users Table
steps.screen:
TSO: BROWSE 'SYS1.SECURE.CNTL(USERS)'

HERC01   ADMIN   *CUL8TR  Y
HERC01   RAKFADM  CUL8TR  Y
HERC02   USER     PASS4U  N
IBMUSER  ADMIN   *SYS1    Y
IBMUSER  RAKFADM *SYS1    Y

Format:
UUUUUUUU GGGGGGGG PPPPPPPP O
Username Group    *Password Operations

* = password never expires
Y = Operations authority (bypass most checks)
N = normal user
steps.expected: Identify all users, their groups, passwords (cleartext!), and Operations flag.
steps.hint: Passwords are stored in CLEARTEXT. If SYS1.SECURE.CNTL is readable, all passwords are exposed.

steps.title: Analyze Operations Authority
steps.screen:
Users with Operations=Y:
  HERC01  — ADMIN group, password CUL8TR
  IBMUSER — ADMIN group, password SYS1

Users with Operations=N:
  HERC02  — USER group, password PASS4U

Operations=Y means:
- Can access ANY resource unless EXPLICITLY denied
- Closest equivalent to 'root' on Unix
- Can be overridden by NONE access in profiles

Risk: Too many Operations=Y users = no accountability
steps.expected: Understand that Operations authority is the highest privilege level in RAKF.
steps.hint: In a real audit, you'd flag every user with Operations=Y and verify they need it.

steps.title: Browse RAKF Profiles Table
steps.screen:
TSO: BROWSE 'SYS1.SECURE.CNTL(PROFILES)'

DATASET *                                          READ
DATASET *                                  ADMIN   ALTER
DATASET *                                  STCGROUPALTER
DATASET SYS1.SECURE.*                              NONE
DATASET SYS1.SECURE.*                      ADMIN   NONE
DATASET SYS1.SECURE.*                      RAKFADM UPDATE
FACILITYDIAG8CMD                                   NONE
FACILITYDIAG8CMD                           ADMIN   READ
FACILITYSVC244                                     NONE
FACILITYSVC244                             ADMIN   READ

Format: CLASS RESOURCE_NAME GROUP ACCESS
steps.expected: Trace the access decision chain: universal access first, then group overrides.
steps.hint: Blank group = universal access (UACC). Specific group entries override UACC.

steps.title: UACC Misconfiguration Risk
steps.screen:
Profile Analysis:

DATASET *    (blank group)   READ

This means: EVERY authenticated user can READ
every dataset on the system by default.

Only specific profiles override this:
  SYS1.SECURE.*  NONE  — blocks everyone
  SYS1.DUPLEX    NONE  — blocks everyone
  SYS1.PAGECSA   NONE  — blocks everyone

But: SYS1.PARMLIB, SYS1.PROCLIB, SYS1.LINKLIB
have NO specific profile = inherit UACC=READ

Any user can READ system configuration!
steps.expected: Identify that the default UACC=READ on DATASET * is overly permissive.
steps.hint: On a production system, UACC should be NONE with explicit grants. TK5 is permissive for education.

steps.title: Facility Class Protection
steps.screen:
Facility profiles control system utilities:

FACILITYSVC244     NONE  — blocks SVC 244 by default
FACILITYSVC244     ADMIN READ — ADMIN can use SVC 244

FACILITYRAKFADM    NONE  — blocks RAKF admin tools
FACILITYRAKFADM    ADMIN NONE — even ADMIN is blocked!
FACILITYRAKFADM    RAKFADM READ — only RAKFADM group

SVC 244: Allows programs to enter supervisor state.
Without FACILITY protection, ANY user could escalate.

RAKFADM: Controls who can reload security tables.
Note: ADMIN group is explicitly DENIED (NONE).
steps.expected: Understand how FACILITY profiles protect system utilities from abuse.
steps.hint: SVC 244 is the classic MVS privilege escalation vector. RAKF's secured version checks FACILITY class.

steps.title: RACF Indicator & Dataset Protection
steps.screen:
RAKF only protects datasets with the RACF indicator set.

To check: CDSCB 'dataset' VOL(vol) UNIT(SYSDA) SHR
To set:   CDSCB 'dataset' VOL(vol) UNIT(SYSDA) SHR RACF
To clear: CDSCB 'dataset' VOL(vol) UNIT(SYSDA) SHR NORACF

WARNING: Due to DADSM bugs in MVS 3.8j, an attacker
can manipulate an UNPROTECTED dataset to bypass RAKF
checks on PROTECTED datasets.

Fix: Set RACF indicator on ALL datasets, not just
sensitive ones. Use VTOCSRAC/VSAMLRAC/VSAMSRAC
utilities to bulk-set indicators.
steps.expected: Understand the RACF indicator requirement and the DADSM bypass vulnerability.
steps.hint: This is a real vulnerability in MVS 3.8j — unprotected datasets can be used to bypass protected ones.

steps.title: Security Audit Summary
steps.screen:
RAKF Security Audit — TK5 Default Configuration

[HIGH] Cleartext passwords in SYS1.SECURE.CNTL
[HIGH] UACC=READ on DATASET * (all datasets readable)
[MED]  2 users with Operations=Y (HERC01, IBMUSER)
[MED]  Default passwords (CUL8TR, SYS1, PASS4U)
[LOW]  PROGRAM class not enforced (MVS 3.8j limitation)
[INFO] SYS1.SECURE.* properly protected (UACC=NONE)
[INFO] SVC 244 protected via FACILITY class
[INFO] RAKFADM restricted to RAKFADM group only

Remediation:
1. Change default passwords
2. Set UACC=NONE on DATASET *, grant READ explicitly
3. Set RACF indicator on all datasets
4. Install ZP60034 for userid propagation in batch
steps.expected: Produce a security assessment of the RAKF configuration.
steps.hint: This mirrors a real mainframe security audit — the same findings apply to production RACF systems.
